claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to gather some information about your circumstances.

Let me start with a basic question:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm interested in finding out if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for your one child, I need to ask you some questions.

**What is your child's name and how old are they?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: My child's name is Alex and they're 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! Since Alex is 17 years old, their eligibility depends on whether they're in approved education or training.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, studying at school, college, or in an approved training program)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. They left school and aren't doing any courses or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a few more questions to fully assess the situation.

**Is Alex currently working? If so, how many hours per week do they work?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have another important question:

**Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services?** (If they've done this within the last 20 weeks since leaving education, you may still be eligible for Child Benefit during that period)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they haven't registered with any careers service or the armed forces. They just left school and that's it.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have a couple more questions to complete my assessment:

**How long ago did Alex leave school or education?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been a few months now since they left school.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

**Do you live in the UK, and does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester. And yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. One final question:

**Is Alex currently receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't receiving any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

**For Alex (17 years old):**
- Child Benefit is available for children under 16, **or** for young people aged 16-19 if they remain in approved education or training
- Since Alex left school several months ago and is not currently in any approved education or training, they don't meet this requirement
- There is an exception that allows Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks after a 16 or 17 year old leaves education, but only if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services - Alex has not done this

**What Alex could do:**
If Alex were to either:
- Return to approved education or training, or
- Register with a careers service

Then you may become eligible for Child Benefit again. If Alex's circumstances change, you would need to make a new claim or contact HMRC to update your situation.

I understand this may not be the outcome you were hoping for. If you have any questions about this decision, please let me know.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and has left school several months ago. According to the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16, or for a child aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training. Alex is not currently in approved education or training. Additionally, there is a provision that allows Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, but Alex has not registered with either. Since Alex left school 'a few months ago' and has not registered with a careers service, the 20-week extension period (approximately 5 months) may have expired or is not available as they haven't registered. Alex is not working 24 hours or more per week and is not receiving benefits in their own right, which would otherwise disqualify them. However, the fundamental issue is that Alex does not meet the criteria of being either under 16 or being 16-19 and in approved education/training. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) with their 17-year-old child Alex. While the claimant would normally be responsible for Alex as they live together, Alex does not meet the age and education requirements for Child Benefit eligibility. Alex left school several months ago and is not in approved education or training, nor has Alex registered with a careers service to access the 20-week extension period. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

While the claimant would normally be responsible for Alex as they live together, Alex does not meet the age and education requirements for Child Benefit eligibility. Alex left school several months ago and is not in approved education or training, nor has Alex registered with a careers service to access the 20-week extension period. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
